questions.prompt: Difficulty both starting actions and ceasing repetitive behaviours after frontal damage was referred to as:
In the apple-tree cost-benefit task discussed under initiation, participants decide whether to act after seeing cues for:
options: Reward magnitude and delay discounting only

questions.prompt: In the Wisconsin Card Sorting Task, frontal patients often know the current sorting rule is wrong yet continue using it. This is best described as:
A task in which the examiner switches the sorting rule from color to number to shape primarily measures:
In the flanker task, an indicator of self-monitoring is:
options: Reduced activity in salience regions after mistakes

questions.prompt: Which pattern is most characteristic of incongruent flanker trials relative to congruent trials?
options: They are faster and more accurate
They are slower and more error-prone
They eliminate the need for monitoring
They depend mainly on episodic memory

questions.prompt: Inhibition is best defined as the ability to:
options: Stop actions that would interfere with the goal

questions.prompt: According to the lecture summary, monitoring relies especially on which regions/network?
options: Hippocampus and temporal pole; default mode network

questions.prompt: Which region/network pairing is most consistent with the lecture’s 'most valuable players' for cognitive control?
As n-back demand increases, activity increases in several top-down attention and cognitive control regions. Which option fits the lecture?
TMS to which region impaired 3-back performance in the lecture example?
A key issue in the maintenance/manipulation debate was that sustained activation during the delay period appeared strongest in:
The Riggal and Postle account ultimately suggested that during the delay period, maintained content is represented in:
options: Sensory regions such as LOC, while fronto-parietal regions control maintenance

questions.prompt: Which statement best fits the activated long-term memory account discussed in lecture?
options: Working memory can only store visual and auditory material
Any sensory modality can be actively maintained through executive influence on high-level sensory regions
Working memory and long-term memory are completely independent
The theory eliminates a role for distraction

questions.prompt: Patient H.M. was especially important because his case suggested that:
options: Short-term memory depends entirely on hippocampus
Only non-declarative memory relies on hippocampus
Declarative memory depends on hippocampus, while short-term and non-declarative memory can remain intact
The hippocampus is irrelevant for memory

questions.prompt: The lecture described the hippocampus as especially adept at:
options: Binding together different sensory and conceptual features of ongoing events

questions.prompt: Reconsolidation refers to the idea that once retrieved, a consolidated memory becomes briefly:
Retrieval was described as relying in part on:
options: The reinstatement of brain patterns present at encoding
Loss of overlap with mental simulation networks
Automatic activation of the HPA axis

questions.prompt: In the HPA axis, hormones released into the blood ultimately influence the release of:
According to the lecture, the hypothalamus receives key threat-related input from the:
The amygdala was described as playing an important role in:
options: Detecting emotional information and learning emotional significance or reward

questions.prompt: Damage to the amygdala would most likely lead to which pattern?
options: Improved detection of angry faces in crowds
Judging unfamiliar faces as more trustworthy
Enhanced attention to the eyes and mouth

questions.prompt: In monkeys, extensive amygdala damage was associated with Kluver-Bucy syndrome, which includes:
options: Indiscriminate ingestion and inappropriate sexual behaviour
Inability to form long-range functional connections

questions.prompt: A neutral stimulus begins to elicit fear after repeated pairing with a noxious stimulus. This paradigm is called:
Theory of mind refers to the ability to:
options: Represent other people’s mental states as separate from one’s own
Encode episodic details into semantic memory

questions.prompt: Which set of regions was highlighted as especially relevant to theory of mind?
options: TPJ, temporal poles, superior temporal sulcus, mPFC

questions.prompt: Empathy was divided into which three subcomponents?
options: Emotional contagion, cognitive perspective taking, and pro-social action

questions.prompt: Synaptogenesis in early development is characterized by a:
options: Small reduction in synapses from the start
Massive roughly ten-fold increase in synapse number
Process limited to the prefrontal cortex

questions.prompt: What happens to synapses over time according to the lecture?
options: Unused synapses strengthen and active synapses are pruned
Active synapses thrive while inactive ones deteriorate and are pruned
All synapses are retained into adulthood

questions.prompt: The lecture emphasized that pruning occurs earliest in which type of region?
Which ordering best matches the developmental progression of pruning across regions?
options: All regions prune at the same time

questions.prompt: At birth, the brain is relatively:
Which region was described as myelinating for a very long time, potentially into the 20s?
options: Corpus callosum and PFC white matter
Primary visual cortex only in infancy

questions.prompt: Experience-expectant systems are best described as neural systems that:
options: Depend on idiosyncratic experiences and are unnecessary for typical development
Respond to nearly universal inputs needed for normal development

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of experience-dependent plasticity from the lecture?
A sensitive period was described as a time when organisms are especially responsive to certain inputs. The lecture’s example was that second-language learning before about age 6 is associated with:
options: Language abilities comparable to native speakers
No later decline in performance with age of arrival
Purely procedural learning with no cortical involvement
